title: Preserve the constraint: balance, solve, and check

objectives.statement: Distinguish expressions from equations and interpret variables, coefficients, constants, and domain constraints.
Solve linear equations with parentheses, fractions, and variables on both sides using equality-preserving operations.
Rearrange a multivariable formula for a requested variable while recording restrictions introduced by division.
Verify candidate solutions by substitution, classify contradictions or identities, and reject values outside the declared domain.

scenes.title: A plausible number that violates its equation
scenes.contentHtml:
A plausible number that violates its equation

A fictional sampler stores 17 baseline records plus 6 records for each interval. Its total is 83 records. A dashboard reports 12 intervals.

The verbal model is

17+6n=83.17+6n=83.

Twelve sounds plausible, but substitution rejects it: 17+6(12)=8917+6(12)=89, not 83. Solving gives 6n=666n=66 and n=11n=11, which checks exactly.

This lesson treats an equation as a constraint. Solving is the controlled process of rewriting that constraint without changing which values satisfy it. The loop is:

  1. represent the relationship and domain;
  2. preserve equality with reversible operations;
  3. isolate the requested variable;
  4. substitute into the original equation and interpret.

Starting check

Check
id check-starting-readiness

Try all five questions before reading the feedback.

  1. Without exact calculation, is (83−17)/6(83-17)/6 closest to 1, 10, or 100? Explain using rounded values.
  2. Is −30/4-30/4 positive or negative, and is its magnitude between 7 and 8?
  3. Is 3x+73x+7 an expression or an equation?
  4. Does adding 4 to only one side preserve equality?
  5. If a variable counts complete intervals, is n=−2.5n=-2.5 admissible even if algebra produces it?

Feedback after your attempt: (83−17)/6(83-17)/6 is closest to 10 because 83−1783-17 is about 60 and 60/6=1060/6=10. The value −30/4-30/4 is negative and lies between −8-8 and −7-7. The form 3x+73x+7 is an expression because it makes no equality assertion. Adding to only one side does not preserve equality. A value of −2.5-2.5 is outside a complete-interval domain even if it emerged from algebra.

If either estimation answer was uncertain, round the numerator before dividing and predict the sign before calculating. If an equation operation was uncertain, write the same operation on two separate lines, one for each side.

Source Note
claims claim-quantity-equation, claim-numerical-equation, claim-unit-independent-rearrangementsources source-nist-si-chapter7

NIST distinguishes quantity equations from unit-dependent numerical-value equations. The sampler model and constraint-first method are original.

Accessibility and alternatives

Every symbolic step is described in words and presented in source order. No task depends on color, spatial “movement,” dragging, or timing. Read 3(x−2)3(x-2) aloud as “three times the quantity x minus two.”

scenes.title: An equation describes a solution set
scenes.contentHtml:
An equation describes a solution set

An expression such as 4x−94x-9 represents a value. An equation such as 4x−9=154x-9=15 asserts that two expressions are equal. Its solution set contains every allowed value that makes the assertion true.

Definition
id definition-equivalent-equations

Equivalent equations have the same solution set. Adding or subtracting the same expression on both sides preserves equality; multiplying or dividing both sides by the same nonzero quantity also preserves it.

For 4x−9=154x-9=15:

4x−9+9=15+9,4x=24,x=6.4x-9+9=15+9,\qquad 4x=24,\qquad x=6.

The final substitution 4(6)−9=154(6)-9=15 confirms the candidate in the original constraint.

Structure before arithmetic

Parentheses group a single factor: 3(x−2)=213(x-2)=21 means the entire difference is tripled. You may divide by 3 first or distribute first; either valid path preserves the solution set.

Fractions can be cleared by multiplying every term on both sides by a common nonzero denominator. For

x3+x4=7,\frac{x}{3}+\frac{x}{4}=7,

multiplying the whole equation by 12 gives 4x+3x=844x+3x=84, so x=12x=12.

Three possible outcomes

  • A statement such as x=5x=5: one solution.
  • A contradiction such as 0=70=7: no solution.
  • An identity such as 0=00=0: every value in the declared domain is a solution.

Domain restrictions remain part of the problem. Division by a variable requires recording that it is nonzero; a count may require a nonnegative integer even when the equation is defined for all real numbers.

Source Note
claims claim-quantity-equation, claim-numerical-equation, claim-unit-independent-rearrangementsources source-nist-si-chapter7

NIST supports the separation between unit-independent quantity relations and numerical-value equations. The solution-set framework and algebraic derivations are original mathematical synthesis.

scenes.title: Solve without changing the constraint
scenes.contentHtml:
Solve without changing the constraint

A fictional counter follows 5(2x−3)+4=3x+315(2x-3)+4=3x+31. Find xx and verify it.

Worked Example
id worked-example-linear-both-sides

1. Declare the domain. No denominator or contextual restriction is stated, so use real numbers.

2. Expose structure. Distribute 5 across both terms:

10x−15+4=3x+31,10x-15+4=3x+31,

so 10x−11=3x+3110x-11=3x+31.

3. Preserve equality. Subtract 3x3x from both sides and add 11 to both sides:

7x=42.7x=42.

Divide both sides by nonzero 7: x=6x=6.

4. Substitute into the original. Left side: 5(12−3)+4=495(12-3)+4=49. Right side: 18+31=4918+31=49. Both sides agree.

5. Challenge the scale. Before exact work, coefficients suggest a small positive value; the checked result is consistent.

Check
id check-original-equation

Why is checking the original equation stronger than checking only the final line 7x=427x=42?

Feedback after your attempt: An earlier distribution or sign error could create a valid solution to the wrong transformed equation. The original retains every initial term and grouping.

Source Note
claims claim-unit-independent-rearrangementsources source-nist-si-chapter7

The equation, derivation, check, and fictional framing are original. The source supports treating symbolic quantity relations independently of chosen numerical units.

scenes.title: Moving a term is shorthand, not magic
scenes.contentHtml:
Moving a term is shorthand, not magic

The phrase “move −9-9 across and change its sign” can hide the operation that preserves equality.

Misconception
id misconception-moving-term

In 4x−9=154x-9=15, the −9-9 does not travel. We add 9 to both sides:

4x−9+9=15+9.4x-9+9=15+9.

The apparent sign change is the simplified result of inverse operations. Writing the operation prevents three common failures: changing only one side, changing a sign without moving the whole term, and applying an operation to only part of a grouped expression.

The same care matters in formula rearrangement. From y=ax+by=ax+b, subtract bb from both sides, then divide by aa only if a≠0a\ne0:

x=y−ba,a≠0.x=\frac{y-b}{a},\qquad a\ne0.
Source Note
claims claim-unit-independent-rearrangementsources source-nist-si-chapter7

The misconception and repair are original. The resulting symbolic rearrangement preserves the source-supported quantity-equation relation without binding it to chosen units.

scenes.title: Fade from operations to justification
scenes.contentHtml:
Fade from operations to justification

Check
id check-faded-practice

Attempt all four tasks before opening or reading the feedback. Record the operation applied to both sides.

  1. Two-step equation: Solve 7x+5=477x+5=47.
  2. Parentheses: Solve 4(x+3)=2x+224(x+3)=2x+22.
  3. Fractions: Solve x/5−(x−2)/3=2x/5-(x-2)/3=2.
  4. Classify: Solve 2(x+4)=2x+82(x+4)=2x+8.

Feedback after all four attempts:

  1. Subtract 5, then divide by 7: x=6x=6. Check: 42+5=4742+5=47.
  2. Distribute first: 4x+12=2x+224x+12=2x+22, so 2x=102x=10 and x=5x=5. Both original sides equal 32.
  3. Multiply every term by 15: 3x−5(x−2)=303x-5(x-2)=30. Then −2x+10=30-2x+10=30, so x=−10x=-10. Original check: −2−(−12/3)=2-2-(-12/3)=2.
  4. Both sides reduce to 2x+82x+8; the result 0=00=0 means every real xx satisfies the identity.

Recovery route

  • Sign error: write the same addition or subtraction explicitly on both sides.
  • Lost term after clearing fractions: multiply every term by the common denominator.
  • Contradiction mistaken for a value: stop when the variable cancels and classify the remaining statement.
  • Candidate fails substitution: return to the first line where transformed sides ceased to be equivalent.
Source Note
claims claim-unit-independent-rearrangementsources source-nist-si-chapter7

All practice equations, solutions, and recovery routes are original.

scenes.title: Rearrange before inserting numbers
scenes.contentHtml:
Rearrange before inserting numbers

A fictional process uses the quantity relation

Q=r(t−t0),Q=r(t-t_0),

where QQ is accumulated output in millilitres (mL), rr is a constant rate in millilitres per minute (mL/min), and tt and t0t_0 are times in minutes (min).

Check
id check-transfer-rate-time

Solve symbolically for tt, then evaluate for Q=126 mLQ=126\ \mathrm{mL}, r=9 mL/minr=9\ \mathrm{mL/min}, and t0=4 mint_0=4\ \mathrm{min}. Before substituting, show why Q/rQ/r can be added to t0t_0.

Feedback after a complete attempt:

Divide by rr only with restriction r≠0r\ne0:

Qr=t−t0.\frac{Q}{r}=t-t_0.

Add t0t_0 to both sides:

t=t0+Qr,r≠0.t=t_0+\frac{Q}{r},\qquad r\ne0.

The quotient Q/rQ/r has units (mL)/(mL/min)=min(\mathrm{mL})/(\mathrm{mL/min})=\mathrm{min}, so it can be added to t0t_0. Substitute only after rearranging: t=4 min+14 min=18 mint=4\ \mathrm{min}+14\ \mathrm{min}=18\ \mathrm{min}.

Check in the original: (9 mL/min)(18 min−4 min)=126 mL(9\ \mathrm{mL/min})(18\ \mathrm{min}-4\ \mathrm{min})=126\ \mathrm{mL}. The rearranged quantity equation remains meaningful across compatible unit choices; the numerical values must use a coherent set of units.

Source Note
claims claim-quantity-equation, claim-numerical-equation, claim-unit-independent-rearrangementsources source-nist-si-chapter7

NIST directly distinguishes quantity equations from unit-dependent numerical equations. The process relation, values, solution, and checking route are original.

scenes.title: Rebuild the preserve-and-check method
scenes.contentHtml:
Rebuild the preserve-and-check method

  1. State the equation, requested variable, and domain.
  2. Expose grouping and denominators.
  3. Apply the same reversible operation to both sides.
  4. Record restrictions before dividing.
  5. Substitute into the original and interpret the result.

Retrieval

  • What distinguishes an expression from an equation?
  • Why does the same nonzero multiplier preserve a solution set?
  • What do 0=70=7 and 0=00=0 mean after a variable cancels?
  • Why should formula rearrangement precede numerical substitution?

Complete the lesson mastery assessment. If the operation trail is unclear, return to the misconception scene. If fractions fail, redo practice item 3 while circling every term. If a numerical answer lacks a domain or substitution check, redo the transfer task.

Source Note
claims claim-quantity-equation, claim-numerical-equation, claim-unit-independent-rearrangementsources source-nist-si-chapter7

The synthesis uses NIST's equation distinctions; the preserve-and-check method is original and claims no measured learner effectiveness.

assessment.title: Algebra and equation solving mastery gate

items.prompt: Which statement is an equation rather than an expression?

answer.summary: An equation asserts equality between expressions.
answer.reasoning: Only option B contains an equality relation; the others represent values without asserting a constraint.
commonErrors: Treating any occurrence of a variable as an equation

answer.reasoning: Subtract 2x and add 7 on both sides: 4x = 28, so x = 7.
commonErrors: Combining terms across equality without applying an operation to both sides
Obtaining a negative value from a sign error

items.prompt: Solve x/4 + (x - 1)/6 = 5. Show how the denominator is cleared and verify in the original equation.

answer.reasoning: The common denominator must multiply every term on both sides; substitution confirms the fractional value.
commonErrors: Multiplying only fractional terms and not the right side

items.prompt: What is the solution set of 3(x - 2) = 3x + 1?

answer.summary: The equation reduces to the contradiction -6 = 1.
answer.reasoning: Distributing gives 3x - 6 = 3x + 1; subtracting 3x leaves a false statement independent of x.
commonErrors: Dividing by a cancelled variable coefficient
Treating every cancellation as an identity

items.prompt: Rearrange A = b(h + k)/2 for h and state every required nonzero restriction.

answer.correct: Multiply by 2: 2A = b(h + k). If b ≠ 0, divide by b and subtract k: h = 2A/b - k. The required division restriction is b ≠ 0.
answer.reasoning: Each operation is applied to both sides; the only new division is by b, so its nonzero restriction must be retained.
commonErrors: Failing to distribute division over the grouped sum

items.prompt: A fictional storage model is T = 2.5n + 18, where n is a nonnegative integer batch count and T is total storage units. For T = 73, solve for n, show equality-preserving steps, verify in the original model, and interpret the domain. Then consider a separate case with T = 74 under the same model and domain: explain why n = 22.4 passes one check but fails another.

answer.correct: For T = 73: 73 = 2.5n + 18. Subtract 18 on both sides: 55 = 2.5n. Divide by 2.5: n = 22. Check: 2.5(22) + 18 = 73. The value is admissible because it is a nonnegative integer. In the separate T = 74 case, 2.5(22.4) + 18 = 74, so 22.4 satisfies the real-valued equation but is not an admissible complete-batch count.
answer.summary: n = 22 batches for T = 73; in the separate T = 74 case, n = 22.4 passes substitution but fails the integer-domain check.
answer.reasoning: Substitution tests whether a candidate satisfies the equation; the declared nonnegative-integer domain is a separate admissibility test. The T = 74 case isolates that distinction because 22.4 passes substitution but fails the domain check.
commonErrors: Dividing 73 by 2.5 before removing the baseline
Testing 22.4 against T = 73 instead of the separate T = 74 case
Ignoring the integer domain after substitution succeeds

criteria.description: Identifies the equality assertion rather than an expression.
Multiplies every term by the common denominator.
Recognizes the contradiction and concludes no solution.
Applies inverse operations to the full equation.
Isolates h in an equivalent form.
Represents T = 73 and isolates n with equality-preserving steps.
Substitutes 22 for T = 73 and 22.4 for the separate T = 74 case.
Interprets 22 as admissible for T = 73 and 22.4 as equation-valid but domain-invalid for the separate T = 74 case.

sources.title: NIST Guide to the SI, Chapter 7: Rules and Style Conventions for Expressing Values of Quantities
creators: National Institute of Standards and Technology
sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
sources.version: Web presentation modified 2025-08-18; based on NIST Special Publication 811
sources.locator: Sections 7.1 and 7.11 on quantity values, quantity equations, and numerical-value equations
sources.license: NIST Technical Series/public information under NIST Copyrights & Disclaimers and technical-series licensing statements, subject to attribution and third-party exceptions

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: The lesson uses only factual distinctions between quantity and numerical-value equations. All algebra explanations, equations, examples, feedback, and assessments are original.

agentAccess.notes: The cited NIST page and NIST copyrights/disclaimers page were rechecked during finalization. No source-specific agent restriction, login, paywall, or access-control barrier was found.

claims.statement: A quantity equation expresses a relationship among quantities and is independent of the units chosen to express those quantities.
claims.scope: Symbolic quantity equations in introductory quantitative science.
claims.uncertainty: Unit independence does not establish that the selected equation is the correct model for a system.

claims.statement: An equation among numerical values depends on the units in which the associated quantities are expressed.
claims.scope: Numerical-value equations tied to explicitly stated compatible units.
claims.uncertainty: The lesson does not teach unit conversion or measurement uncertainty.

claims.statement: Algebraically equivalent rearrangements of a valid quantity equation retain the same quantity relationship when operations are defined and restrictions such as nonzero divisors are preserved.
claims.scope: Equality-preserving elementary algebra over real-valued quantities with declared domains.
claims.uncertainty: Nonreversible operations can introduce or remove candidate solutions and require additional checking.

terms.definition: A combination of numbers, variables, and operations that represents a value but does not assert equality.
A statement asserting that two expressions are equal.
All values in the declared domain that make an equation true.
Equations with the same solution set over the declared domain.
An equation true for every value in its declared domain.
A condition limiting allowed variable values, such as a nonzero denominator or a nonnegative integer count.

Originality, evidence, and rights

The preserve-and-check structure, sampler opening, counter equation, misconception repair, process transfer, all values, explanations, feedback, recovery routes, and assessments are original. All applied settings are fictional. NIST Chapter 7 supplies only factual authority for the distinction between quantity equations and unit-dependent numerical-value equations. No NIST expression, example, table, or image is copied or adapted.

The cited NIST page and NIST copyrights/disclaimers page were rechecked on 2026-07-23. No agent restriction, login, paywall, or access-control barrier was found. No third-party assets, datasets, personal data, or patient material are included.

This mathematical lesson offers no clinical advice and makes no claim of review, approval, publication, measured effectiveness, credit, or competence.
